metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Testing section whether `test_enrich_quality` (a) calls the live LLM and asserts deterministic recall, or (b) loads pre-stored enrich output fixtures and checks gold membership offline — and state the fixture directory structure (e.g., one JSON per ticket: `{body: ..., digest: {...}, gold: [...]}`).
findings.finding: The plan never specifies whether `test_enrich_quality` calls a live LLM or uses pre-stored LLM response fixtures. It claims the test is 'DETERMINISTIC' (implying pre-stored), yet the gold-entity check must be against actual enrich output — if pre-stored responses are used, the fixtures must already contain the gold entities (i.e., the fixtures ARE the LLM responses); if live, the test is non-deterministic despite the claim. An executor cannot implement this test without resolving this fork.
findings.location: Testing section — `test_enrich_quality` paragraph; AC line 'Quality fixture test `test_enrich_quality`'
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'Each fixture file is a single JSON object `{ticket_body: string, stored_digest: ticket_digest, gold: string[]}`. The test loads `stored_digest` from file (no LLM call) and asserts `len(set(gold) & (set(stored_digest.key_entities) | set(stored_digest.problem_keywords))) >= 1`. Fixtures are committed to the repo and regenerated offline via `scripts/regen_enrich_fixtures.py` when the prompt changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `test_enrich_quality` uses a live LLM call (with appropriate CI gating) or a canned fixture response; if canned, explicitly document that the fixture responses are sourced from real model outputs (not hand-authored to match gold sets), and add a note that prompt-quality regressions are out of scope for this test.
findings.finding: The `test_enrich_quality` quality fixture test is described as producing 'DETERMINISTIC assertions,' implying it uses canned/pre-baked LLM responses rather than a live model call. If the fixture LLM responses are authored by the same person who defines the gold entity sets, the test becomes a self-authored oracle: it verifies that the op can parse a hand-crafted response that was built to contain the gold entities, not that the prompt actually elicits correct extraction from a real model. The plan does not clarify whether the quality test invokes a live LLM or uses a fixture response.
findings.location: Testing section — `test_enrich_quality` description; AC: `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_enrich_quality`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'The fixture LLM responses in tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/ are captured from real Anthropic API calls (recorded once, committed); the quality test replays these recorded responses through the full enrich.py pipeline and asserts gold-set membership. Prompt regressions are caught by re-recording fixtures when the prompt changes.' This converts the test from a potential self-authored oracle to a regression-snapshot with explicit provenance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` to the Scope edit targets and specify how `ticket_digest` is exempted from the `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` guard — either by adding a `TICKET_DIGEST` constant wired into `CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` (it is a prompt output contract, like `code_review_base_output`) or by adding it to `OUTPUT_SCHEMAS` if the op's output is a public structured surface.
findings.finding: The plan adds `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_digest.schema.json` to disk but does NOT wire it into `schemas.OUTPUT_SCHEMAS`, `schemas.INPUT_SCHEMAS`, or `schemas.CONTRACT_SCHEMAS`. The existing `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` coverage-guard test (tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py:37-50) asserts that every `.schema.json` file in `src/rebar/schemas/` is referenced in one of those sets — the test will fail as soon as `ticket_digest.schema.json` lands on disk, blocking CI.
findings.location: Scope section / 'Output schema `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_digest.schema.json`' + `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` (not mentioned)
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py`, add `TICKET_DIGEST = 'ticket_digest'` and include it in `CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` (analogous to `'code_review_base_output'`, which is also a prompt structured-output contract — not a command `--output`). This satisfies `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` without registering the schema as a CLI command output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the modeling reference from `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` to `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (or `plan_review/passes.py`), which actually demonstrates the `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")` pattern the plan intends.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` is the implementation model demonstrating `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`, but a full read of that file shows it contains NO `RunRequest` construction and NO `single_turn` usage — it delegates entirely to `gate_dispatch.produce_completion_verdict` via the v3 workflow engine. The actual codebase exemplar for the `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")` pattern is `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (line ~157–165) and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py` (multiple sites). An implementer following the plan's guidance will look in `completion.py` and find no usable pattern.
findings.location: ## What — 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` (assemble context → load prompt → `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` (assemble context → load prompt → `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`)'  With: 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (lines ~157–165) or `plan_review/passes.py` (e.g. the `PASS_NOVElTY` call site), which demonstrate the `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn", output_schema=<name>)` pattern. Note: `completion.py` does NOT use RunRequest directly — it dispatches via the v3 workflow engine.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the error contract for malformed structured output in AC5 — e.g., 'raises `rebar.llm.errors.EnrichParseError` (a subclass of `ValueError`)' — so the test assertion and downstream callers have a concrete type to target.
findings.finding: AC5 ('Malformed structured output → tolerant-parse clean error, no partial write') and the corresponding test description ('FakeRunner malformed → clean error') leave the error contract unspecified: is 'clean error' a typed exception raise, a None/sentinel return, a logged-and-swallowed error, or a Result type? Without this, the test assertion and the op's caller contract are ambiguous.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 line; Testing section `test_bad_json` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'raises `EnrichParseError(ticket_id, raw_response)` — a typed exception defined in `enrich.py`, subclassing `ValueError`; `test_bad_json` uses `pytest.raises(EnrichParseError)`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify, in the **Scope** or **What** section, exactly which `show_ticket` fields are assembled into the `single_turn` `instructions` argument (e.g. title, description, comments) and in what format (markdown rendering vs. raw JSON), so `test_logs_excluded` and `test_enrich_quality` have a deterministic contract to test against.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how the ticket content is assembled into the `single_turn` RunRequest's `instructions` argument — which fields of `show_ticket` output are included, how they are serialized (markdown? JSON? raw text?), and whether the assembly itself trims logs/code before the prompt sees them. The plan says 'logs/stack-traces/code discarded by the prompt,' but a ~4000-token ticket body with a full stack trace embedded may overflow the useful portion of the context window before the prompt can discard it, and a test named `test_logs_excluded` asserts on the assembled output — making context assembly load-bearing for both quality and a named AC.
findings.location: ## What / Digest schema section; AC `::test_logs_excluded`; AC `test_enrich_quality`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Scope section, e.g.: 'Context assembly: call `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id)` and render `title + description + comments` as markdown (mirroring `operations.py::_format_ticket`); raw log lines / code blocks are NOT stripped at the assembly layer — the prompt is responsible for instructing the model to discard them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a processing-order specification to the Scope section: state explicitly (a) whether the op calls `finalize_outcome` then post-processes its dict, or builds its own pipeline (like `completion.py`) calling `validate_structured` manually after truncation; and (b) that the final `validate_structured` call must see the already-truncated payload so the stored `ticket_digest` is valid post-truncation.
findings.finding: The plan defines a two-layer output shape: the LLM produces `ticket_digest` (four fields), which is post-processed by the op (truncation/flag), and the op returns `{digest: <ticket_digest>, low_proposition_count: bool}`. The `register_contract('ticket_digest', <builder>)` Pydantic model validates the four-field shape. But `finalize_outcome(mode='structured')` calls `validate_structured(data, output_schema)` and then merges provenance fields (`runner`, `model`, `trace_id`) into the result dict. The plan does not specify at which point in this pipeline `validate_structured` is called relative to the proposition-truncation post-processing step — if the op truncates AFTER `finalize_outcome` returns, truncation happens on an already-validated dict and `validate_structured` never sees the truncated payload; if truncation happens BEFORE, the op must call `validate_structured` manually (as `completion.py` does at line 238). This decision rule is not co-located with the schema definition.
findings.location: Config-bound threading section: 'if the model returns MORE than max, truncate to max; if FEWER than min, keep them and set low_proposition_count=True on the OP'S RETURN object'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC #1 with the 'What'/'Config-bound threading' sections: either restate AC #1 as 'the `digest` field of the op return validates against the `ticket_digest` schema' and name `test_schema_valid` accordingly, or change the body to say the op returns a bare digest and surfaces `low_proposition_count` via a separate mechanism.
findings.finding: AC #1 states `enrich(ticket_id)` 'returns a ticket_digest-valid payload', but the 'What' and 'Config-bound threading' sections explicitly specify the op returns a wrapper `{digest: <ticket_digest>, low_proposition_count: bool}` — a four-plus-one object, NOT a bare ticket_digest. These cannot both be true: a validator applied to the wrapper will fail on `low_proposition_count`; applied to the sub-field it passes but doesn't match the AC wording.
findings.location: AC #1 vs 'What' section / 'Config-bound threading' section
findings.suggested_fix: Reword AC #1 to: '`rebar.llm.enrich(ticket_id)` returns `{digest, low_proposition_count: bool}` where `digest` validates against the `ticket_digest` schema; `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_schema_valid` asserts `validate(result["digest"])` passes.' Update `test_schema_valid` description in Testing section to match.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `'enrich'` to the `__all__` list in `src/rebar/llm/__init__.py` alongside the import of `enrich` from `rebar.llm.enrich`.
findings.finding: The plan says to 'export enrich from src/rebar/llm/__init__.py' but does not mention adding it to the __all__ list. The existing __init__.py has an explicit, maintained __all__ with 30+ named symbols (confirmed at lines 64-106). An import added to the module body but omitted from __all__ will not be re-exported by wildcard import and will be invisible to tools that rely on __all__ for API surface.
findings.location: Scope section: 'export enrich from src/rebar/llm/__init__.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"enrich"` to the `# operations` section of `__all__` in `src/rebar/llm/__init__.py`, parallel to the existing `"review_ticket"`, `"verify_completion"`, etc. entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where and when `register_contract('ticket_digest', builder)` is called to guarantee registration before `response_model_for('ticket_digest')` is first invoked — either move the builder+call into `contracts.py` (matching the built-in pattern), or add a `register_contracts()` call at the bottom of `enrich.py` and document that `__init__.py`'s import of `enrich` is the bootstrap trigger.
findings.finding: The plan places register_contract('ticket_digest', builder) inside enrich.py but does not specify a bootstrapping mechanism that guarantees the contract is registered before response_model_for('ticket_digest') is called. All existing contracts are either defined directly in contracts.py at module load (review_result, completion_verdict at lines 90-91) or are registered by a register_contracts() call at the bottom of their own subpackage module (plan_review/passes.py:213, code_review/contracts.py:133). If a caller imports rebar.llm.contracts without first importing rebar.llm.enrich (e.g. the runner importing contracts directly), response_model_for('ticket_digest') silently falls back to the findings model.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Output schema ... register in src/rebar/llm/contracts.py via register_contract("ticket_digest", <builder>) — the builder lives beside the op in enrich.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the plan_review/passes.py pattern: define the builder in enrich.py, then add a module-level call `contracts.register_contract('ticket_digest', _ticket_digest_model)` at the bottom of enrich.py. Since __init__.py imports enrich, this registration fires whenever rebar.llm is imported — matching how plan_review/passes.py:213 self-registers at import time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Testing or Scope section an explicit statement that the ≥6 quality fixture files are committed to the repo as part of this story's deliverables, or name a seed/generation script that must be run before the test suite.
findings.finding: The proving command `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py -q` will fail if `tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/` does not exist and is not populated before the test runs, yet the plan never specifies how these fixture files are created or that they are committed to the repo — a prerequisite-state ordering gap (verify-command defect taxonomy #7).
findings.location: Testing section — 'fixtures at `tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/`'; Proving command 'python -m pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py -q'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a deliverable bullet: 'Commit ≥6 fixture files to `tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/` (format: `{ticket_body, stored_digest, gold}` JSON) as part of this PR; `test_enrich_quality` is a pure offline load-and-assert test with no live LLM dependency.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the **What** / **Scope** section that `enrich` does NOT need `gate_source.gate_read_root` or snapshot materialization (since it is `single_turn` with no file tools), even though it is otherwise modeled on `completion.py`.
findings.finding: The plan says `enrich` is 'Modeled on `completion.py`', but `completion.py` wraps every call in `gate_source.resolve_gate_handle` + `gate_source.gate_read_root` because it runs an agentic verifier that reads repository files. The `enrich` op is `single_turn` with no file tools — it sends a ticket body and asks for structured output. If the implementer copies the gate wrapper verbatim, every `enrich` call will attempt to materialize an attested snapshot (a git fetch + filesystem copy) for a call that reads no files, adding significant latency and a potential failure mode (no git remote = attested materialization fails). If the implementer omits it, the `assert_gated` guard is never triggered for `single_turn` anyway, so correctness is preserved — but the plan's 'modeled on completion.py' framing creates an ambiguity.
findings.location: ## What — 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py`' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'Unlike `completion.py`, `enrich` does NOT wrap in `gate_source.gate_read_root` — it is `single_turn` with no file tools and requires no snapshot gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC item (or a sub-check in an existing test) that verifies (a) `from rebar.llm import enrich` succeeds and (b) `contracts.get_contract('ticket_digest')` returns a non-None schema after import, ensuring both __init__.py export and contracts.py registration are covered.
findings.finding: Two concrete Scope deliverables — the `enrich` export from `src/rebar/llm/__init__.py` and the `register_contract("ticket_digest", ...)` call in `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` — have no covering acceptance criterion. If either is omitted by the implementer, no AC will catch the regression.
findings.location: Scope section ('export enrich from src/rebar/llm/__init__.py'; 'register in src/rebar/llm/contracts.py') vs Acceptance Criteria section (no corresponding criterion)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test_schema_valid` (or add a `test_public_surface` test) that does `from rebar.llm import enrich` and calls `rebar.llm.contracts.get_contract('ticket_digest')`, asserting non-None. Add a corresponding sub-bullet to AC #1 or a new AC item.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `enrich.py` must auto-register `ticket_digest` at module bottom (like `plan_review/passes.py` and `code_review/contracts.py` do), AND that the `rebar.llm.__init__` export of `enrich` must unconditionally import the module (triggering the registration) so any caller of `rebar.llm.enrich(...)` is guaranteed the contract is registered before `response_model_for` is called.
findings.finding: The `register_contract('ticket_digest', <builder>)` call lives in `enrich.py` (per the plan), but no module that is unconditionally imported at startup (like `contracts.py`, `__init__.py`, or `plan_review/passes.py`) imports `enrich.py`. Every existing contract that isn't in `contracts.py` itself is bootstrapped by its own module's module-level call (e.g. `plan_review/passes.py:213: register_contracts()`, `code_review/contracts.py:133: register_contracts()`), which is triggered only when those modules are imported. If `enrich.py` is not imported before the runner calls `contracts.response_model_for('ticket_digest')`, the lookup silently falls through to the `findings` model (not a `KeyError`), and `finalize_outcome` validates against the wrong schema — the digest is never validated, and the op returns findings-shaped data instead of a digest.
findings.location: Scope section: 'register in src/rebar/llm/contracts.py via register_contract("ticket_digest", <builder>) — the builder lives beside the op in enrich.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit sentence in the Scope section: 'Add `register_contract("ticket_digest", _ticket_digest_model)` at module bottom of `enrich.py` (mirroring `plan_review/passes.py:213`), so importing `enrich.py` always registers the contract as a side-effect. The `from rebar.llm.enrich import enrich` line in `__init__.py` ensures the registration fires for any `rebar.llm.enrich(...)` caller.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retry/backoff policy (e.g., exponential backoff on 429/529, configurable timeout) to the `enrich` op's LLM call, and document the circuit-breaker or degraded-mode behavior when Anthropic is persistently unavailable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new external LLM call in `enrich.py` but specifies no retry, backoff, or circuit-breaker for transient Anthropic failures (rate limits, 529s, timeouts). The only failure mode addressed is 'absent LLM → clean raise', which covers a missing client but not transient network/API errors.
findings.location: ## What / `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py` / AC: `test_absent_llm`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `enrich.py` emits at minimum a structured log entry on each call (ticket_id, success/failure, token count if available, latency) and documents how failures are surfaced to operators.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new LLM call failure point in `enrich.py` but specifies no observability instrumentation (no logs, metrics, or traces). When enrichment fails silently or is slow, operators have no signal to detect or debug the issue.
findings.location: ## What / `src/rebar/llm/enrich.py` / ## Testing

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing (1) why proposition bounds are enforced in the op rather than the schema builder (zero-arg constraint), (2) the Cupid pattern rationale, and (3) the invariant that `low_proposition_count` lives on the op return, not the persisted schema.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two novel architectural decisions — the Cupid/LLM-as-feature-extractor pattern and the config-bound threading workaround (zero-arg builder cannot see config, so proposition bounds live on the op return object rather than the schema) — but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.location: ## Config-bound threading (resolves the zero-arg-builder gap) / ## What
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-cupid-enrich-op.md` with sections: Motivation (Cupid pattern), Decision (op-level bound enforcement), Consequences (low_proposition_count on return object only), and the zero-arg builder constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `overlap_propositions_min/max` need to be `LLMConfig` fields + env vars rather than module-level constants in `enrich.py` (matching the `_VERIFY_MIN_STEPS` / `_REVIEW_MIN_STEPS` precedent), since no other op or story is identified as a consumer of these knobs.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new LLMConfig fields (overlap_propositions_min/max) and two new env vars (REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX) for bounds that are consumed by exactly one operation (enrich.py). The existing codebase treats per-op numeric floors as module-level constants — _VERIFY_MIN_STEPS=480 in completion.py and _REVIEW_MIN_STEPS=120 in operations.py — not as LLMConfig fields. Elevating enrich-only proposition bounds to operator-visible env vars proliferates the config surface without a multi-consumer justification.
findings.location: ## Config-bound threading section; ## Scope → 'Config field target: src/rebar/llm/config.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Define `_ENRICH_PROPOSITIONS_MIN = 2` and `_ENRICH_PROPOSITIONS_MAX = 6` as module-level constants in `enrich.py`, as done for `_VERIFY_MIN_STEPS` in `completion.py`. Promote to `LLMConfig` only if a second op or a documented operator use case emerges.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC or Scope line specifying that `enrich.py` must construct its `RunRequest` with `mode='structured'` and `execution_mode='single_turn'` set directly on the request (not inferred from prompt front-matter), mirroring `completion.py`'s explicit field usage.
findings.finding: The plan says `enrich.py` is "modeled on `completion.py`" (assemble context → load prompt → `RunRequest(mode='structured', execution_mode='single_turn')`) but does not explicitly state that the op must set `mode='structured'` and `execution_mode='single_turn'` on the `RunRequest` directly. The `execution_mode: single_turn` in the prompt front-matter is only acted upon by `workflow/runs.py:build_agent_step_request()`, which is invoked through the workflow-YAML path. Direct-call ops like `enrich()` build their own `RunRequest` (as `completion.py` does with explicit fields) — if the implementer instead follows the simpler `operations.py` pattern (no `mode`/`execution_mode` on `RunRequest`, both defaulting to `'findings'`/`'agentic'`), the single-turn structured path never fires.
findings.location: What section: 'Modeled on src/rebar/llm/completion.py (assemble context → load prompt → RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn") → validate)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive framing requirement to the prompt spec: the `enrich.md` prompt should instruct the model to 'extract the observable symptom or broken behaviour as a plain-English statement' (positive) rather than only 'discard logs/stack-traces' (negative), to avoid the pink-elephant antipattern.
findings.finding: The plan specifies log/stack-trace exclusion entirely as a negative prompt instruction ('logs/stack-traces/code discarded by the prompt'), with no positive framing of what the prompt should extract instead. This is a pink-elephant antipattern: the model is instructed what NOT to include rather than what TO include, which empirically causes the excluded content to appear in outputs. The quality test `test_logs_excluded` catches failures after the fact but does not prevent the antipattern at prompt-authoring time.
findings.location: What section: 'logs/stack-traces/code discarded by the prompt'; Testing section: '::test_logs_excluded (stack-trace frames absent from digest)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the prompt's What section, change the instruction from 'discard logs/stack-traces/code' to 'convert observable symptoms (error messages, failure modes) into plain-English propositions; omit raw stack frames, log lines, and code snippets.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
